title: CMMC Level 2 practice evidence — CUI control area 2

compliance.assurance_rationale: CMMC AC.L2-3.1.5 Least privilege — UC area 2 surfaces CUI-marked systems requiring privilege review, feeding the least-privilege practice for DoD contractors.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that CMMC AC.L2-3.1.5 (Least privilege) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.20.2: CMMC Level 2 practice evidence — CUI control area 2.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.20.2' running on WinEventLog:Security, CrowdStrike FDR, Defender for Office 365, NetFlow, encryption posture exports, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: `WinEventLog:Security`, CrowdStrike FDR, Defender for Office 365, NetFlow, encryption posture exports
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for CrowdStrike FDR (5082), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Office 365 (4055), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). (Splunkbase)
spl:
index=wineventlog sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security earliest=-7d EventCode=4624
| eval uc_tag="22.20.2"
| lookup cui_hosts.csv ComputerName OUTPUT cui_flag
| where cui_flag="true" AND Logon_Type IN ("10","2")
| stats count by Account_Name, IpAddress
description: Demonstrates contractor cybersecurity hygiene for "CMMC Level 2 practice evidence — CUI control area 2" using CUI-scoped telemetry mapped to CMMC 2.0 Level 2 expectations.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for CMMC compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes wineventlog and sourcetypes WinEventLog:Security. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Confirm field extractions and CIM tags for the sourcetypes in scope; (2) Load or maintain the referenced lookups/KVStore collections with owner attestation dates; (3) Schedule the search at an interval aligned to the control’s materiality; (4) Route positive findings to the compliance ticketing queue with required evidence fields; (5) Retain scheduled search exports per records management and legal hold procedures. Indexes required: `index=wineventlog`.
visualization: Stacked bar, Table, Time chart, Single value. Key fields: `Account_Name`, `IpAddress`, `uc_tag`.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.action, Authentication.user, Authentication.src span=1d | sort - count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for the Authentication data model (Authentication.Authentication).
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase app ID 742) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `wineventlog` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `cui_hosts.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('cui_hosts.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `AC.L2-3.1.5`. The control objective is: "Evidence that CMMC AC.L2-3.1.5 (Least privilege) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.20.2: CMMC Level 2 practice evidence — CUI control area 2." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase app ID 742), landing events in `wineventlog` under `WinEventLog:Security`. Security channel alone produces 2000-8000 events per domain-joined host per day depending on audit policy (~2-8 GB/host/month). Domain controllers generate 10x more.

Configure the input through Splunk_TA_windows/local/inputs.conf on each Universal Forwarder. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[WinEventLog://Security]
disabled = 0
index = windows
renderXml = true
current_only = 0
evt_resolve_ad_obj = 1
checkpointInterval = 5
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `EventCode, Account_Name, Account_Domain, Logon_Type, Source_Network_Address, Workstation_Name, Process_Name, TargetUserName, Subject_Account_Name`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=wineventlog earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Confirm field extractions and CIM tags for the sourcetypes in scope. CIM-compliance tagging must be validated end-to-end: a downstream datamodel that does not accelerate because of a missing tag is a silent failure.

• **Hint 2:** Load or maintain the referenced lookups/KVStore collections with owner attestation dates. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

• **Hint 3:** Schedule the search at an interval aligned to the control’s materiality. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** Route positive findings to the compliance ticketing queue with required evidence fields. Routing targets (SOC, compliance team, specific queue) must be documented with named on-call owners; an 'alerts go to the team' runbook fails at 3am on Sunday.

• **Hint 5:** Retain scheduled search exports per records management and legal hold procedures. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "CMMC Level 2 practice evidence — CUI control area 2," the aggregation is the evidence artifact itself — auditors will read these rows as the official record of CMMC control activity.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `cui_hosts.csv` — the enrichment table that adds compliance-relevant business context to raw events. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`cui_flag`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `ComputerName`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `cui_hosts.csv` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval ComputerName = lower(ComputerName)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the last 7 days.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Authentication` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.action, Authentication.user, Authentication.src span=1d | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Authentication` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.20.2: CMMC Level 2 practice evidence — CUI control area 2]
cron_schedule = 0 2 * * 1
dispatch.earliest_time = -7d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.20.2,reg=CMMC
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=wineventlog sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `Account_Name`, `IpAddress`, `uc_tag`. Run:

```spl
index=wineventlog sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table Account_Name, IpAddress, uc_tag
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `cui_hosts.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup cui_hosts.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.20.2: CMMC evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Stacked Bar (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Drill-through table (the key fields):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 3 — Time trend (the key fields):** Place in the main content row. Use `timechart span=1d` aggregated appropriately for the regulation's review cadence (daily for PCI/HIPAA, weekly for ISO-style controls). Annotate the chart with control-change dates so auditors can see when mitigations took effect.

**Panel 4 — Single-value KPI (the key fields):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'CMMC current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** Check the lookup's case sensitivity (Splunk lookups are case-sensitive by default) and null handling — a null on the input side produces a silent miss, not an error.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**CMMC-specific failure modes:**

• Level 1/2/3 distinction: CMMC 2.0 has three levels (Foundational/Advanced/Expert). Detections must specify level — Level 1 is self-assessed against basic cyber hygiene; Level 3 requires NIST SP 800-172 enhanced controls plus triennial assessment.

• FCI vs CUI boundary: CMMC applies only to systems handling Federal Contract Information (FCI) or Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI). Detections scoped to 'entire organisation' fire on out-of-boundary systems, wasting audit effort.

• POA&M acceptance limitation: unlike FISMA, CMMC limits POA&M use — certain controls cannot have open POA&M items at assessment. Detections must know which controls can have open POA&M and which cannot.

• C3PAO assessment cadence: Level 2+ requires third-party assessment every 3 years. Detections measuring control stability must span the full triennial cycle; short-window detections miss drift between assessments.
grandmaExplanation: We keep evidence for CUI control area 2. CMMC sets the bar for protecting sensitive information, and we keep evidence for the level we target.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'CMMC Level 2 practice evidence — CUI control area 2' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper CMMC control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the CMMC control objective.

evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with CMMC control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the CMMC programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
